title: Closing Bank Accounts After a Death
summary: Closing bank accounts involves more nuance than a single phone call — knowing the basics prevents frustrating back-and-forth.
category: After a Loss III: Estate & Digital
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Closing Bank Accounts After a Death | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Closing bank accounts involves more nuance than a single phone call — knowing the basics prevents frustrating back-and-forth.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Individual accounts typically freeze until the estate is settled or an executor is confirmed.
Joint accounts usually pass to the surviving co-owner more simply.
Banks generally require a certified death certificate and proof of authority.

paragraphs: An account solely in the deceased's name is typically frozen once the bank is notified, pending confirmation of the executor or completion of probate, depending on state law and account size — this protects the estate but can feel slow.
Joint accounts with a surviving co-owner usually transfer more simply, often just requiring notification and a death certificate, since the surviving owner already has legal rights to the funds.

nextSteps: Notify the bank with a certified death certificate as a first step.
Ask specifically what proof of authority (executor paperwork) they require.
Clarify the difference in process for joint versus individual accounts.
disclaimer: General guidance — each bank's specific policies and state law both affect the exact process.
